title: Romy Weinberg -Hiring Manager Screen 2 - Panzer | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Romy Weinberg -Hiring Manager Screen 2 - Panzer | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hello.
Hello.
Camera coming momentarily.
I'm just choking on some coffee apparently first.
Oh.
Put that in my schedule.
Hi, how are you?
Hi, I'm fine, thanks.
How are you besides choking?
Yeah, yeah, fine, yeah.
Yeah.
Yes, pleased to meet you.
My name's Matthew.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
That means pragmatically I'm in charge of like content quality and how it translates.
Obviously at the, you know, the client deliverables juncture, like, hey, is this good?
Like, how are we feeling about quality coming out of our pipelines and out of the tooling that we've built and working with the people that ensure that, which is the content managers or CMs.
sense.
Yeah.
To make sure that they're maintaining editorial, you know, standards, et cetera.
But then backwards from there, it's about the quality of output that our workflows and pipelines are generating.
You know, what is, what, what quality are they producing?
What is the delta between there and ideal?

And how do we articulate that either with engineering or prompt engineering, which would be something that the content managers would be involved in or context engineering, which is like an ME combination, ME-CM job, which is all about taking the context that we know about the company or that we're feeding the pipelines and making sure that it is complete, correct, architected properly in order to give it the good raw material it needs to actually then produce the content.
So kind of all of those moving parts and some leadership stuff as well.
Previous to this, I was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch for about 10 years.
So written editorial operations, news operations, disrupts, 11 other events every year.
Um, newsletters, morning shows, uh, a startup competition, ad nauseum, et cetera.
So a lot of content experience over that time.
Um, and I've done some marketing and startups as well.
So I just wanted to give a snapshot of me and I'm happy to hear about you.
Well, um, I have a similar background to you.
have a lot of content experience, which you probably gathered from, I assume looking at my resume and maybe my LinkedIn.
Um, so I've worked at, I started my career in print journalism.
Um, and I've worked at many magazines and moved up through the ranks from editorial assistant at Vanity Fair, Graydon Carter's assistant, which was a very fun job.
Very fun.
Yeah.
I would love to talk to you about that sometime.
I'm sure.
Yeah.
Um, yeah.
Uh, have you read his new, uh, memoir?
You know, it's so funny.
I've read two really delicious reviews of it.
Um, one by a former ex boss, uh, who happened to work there.
at some point too.
And it's gotten me really enticed and I have it in my Kindle, but I haven't gotten a chance to read it.
Well, I would say the reviews have been very entertaining.
I actually listened to the book in like an article just because I worked for him for a long time and I knew him very well personally.
So I kind of just wanted to hear him talking in my ear, which was a, I would say a more, a more enjoyable experience than I think reading it would have been.
Nice.
That's just my, that's my two cents.
But anyway, that was, that was, that was the first stop in my career.
And then somewhere along the way, I decided, hey, this magazine thing may not be around forever.
I think I should try digital.
And I went to work for, well, I worked for, I was working for Martha Stewart at the time that the digital kind of started.

And I built a couple of her core areas on her website and learned all about content marketing there actually, because my role was very hybrid editorial and marketing because we were, we were marketing her products, which she was selling a lot of stuff and she was everywhere.
And we were, you know, you.
Using the web to sort of figure out, well, how do we use this platform to sell her stuff, to promote her, to distribute her content?
So I learned a ton about digital there.
And then went full on digital, worked for AOL, worked for Conde Nast back just in digital, was managing editor of CondeNet for a while.
And then took a freelance stint for a while, which I still do, and worked for Google for a time where Google bought Zagat Restaurant Reviews.
And so we started to...
Oh, I remember that, yeah.

Yeah, so I was on the team that, you know, it was still called Zagat, but at some point we were, what we were doing was really creating the like nascent Google Maps content, which was really cool because I learned how to write those really short snippets of like stuff that people look up on their phones.
And like, it was very specific discipline and it was good.
And then fast forward, my most recent role was at TopTal, which is a tech network, I don't know, you may have heard of it, global company.
So I learned all about working for a global distributed, you know, workforce remotely.
And that was a great experience, three and a half, three years, and was their content managing.
I was their lead editor of content marketing.
So I had started on a website that they own called staffing.com.
I was the lead editor of that.
Then they asked me to take on more, then they asked me to take on events.
So you were mentioning TechCrunch events.

So I was going to a lot of tech events and coming up with content that, you know, went through the full funnel, like distribution and, you know, sending it out to people, trying to get it read, trying to figure out what they wanted, trying to get thought leadership baked into it.
So kind of touched on a lot of different types of content throughout my career.
My most recent role at TopTal really gave me a flavor for more project management and more.
like going going you.

More aligned with the description of this job that I'm interviewing with you for more project management, more managing editor type of stuff, increasing the types of content we were doing, leading efforts through building workflows in Asana to support new and different workflows and work types of content that we were producing, large teams.
And so that's kind of in a nutshell what I've done.
Yeah, yeah.
It is.
And that's great.
I mean, look, the job, especially like the core of what we're doing at the moment is largely content related.
There are elements of it that are process related as well.
And like the, the experience that you've had is, I don't think that there's any part of it that isn't applicable, you know, I mean, working with teams.
Working with writers, distribution, understanding what good content is, working with content of variable lengths, you know, all of that.
Because the client services business is weird, you know, it's elastic in terms of both demand and deliverable style, you know.

We may be producing something that's like a directory of content, you know, that's more of like a programmatic effort where we are populating a directory with content or with data sources plus content one minute for one client.
And then the next may be standard editorial thought leadership fair, top of the funnel stuff, you know, that kind of thing.

And so like there's that's that variance is well suited to people who have had a varied editorial background as opposed to folks who were like, oh, you know, I worked in this one kind of arena for a very long time.
It's not nothing against their skill set, but it does not prepare you for sort of.
The onslaught of, like, variants in content spec and remit and the job that the content is supposed to be doing.
You know, if you've worked with content of different kinds, you know, like, hey, the content has to match the job that it is supposed to accomplish.
And if people have only worked in one mode, it can sometimes be jarring to, like, you know, to switch those contexts.
Talk to me a little bit about, like, team management and, like, working with writers and that sort of thing.
Because the, just to give you a snapshot of the structure, we view the workflows and pipelines, which, let me quickly define those for you.
So, like, the pipelines are any process that we create to generate an output or result for a client from beginning to end.
That means, hey, we've got an idea or let me just take it, boil it down to a really simple use case, which is a post, an article that we want to publish on their site.
In, the input would be a type.
And then the other inputs may be something like simple directions or, you know, directionality or instructions we want to give in the pipeline.
And then the progression through the pipeline is a series of workflows.
So each of the steps in the pipeline we would refer to as a workflow.
And those workflows may perform multiple actions per workflow.
So it's nested.
But the pipeline is really like, hey, here's the steps I'm doing to accomplish this goal.
And then the workflows is what is the job involved in each step?
This is not extremely complex.
I just want to explain it so that when I say workflow or pipeline, you're able to frame it for yourself.
So we view the pipelines as our writers.
You know, the ability to generate content out of them that is factually accurate, the copy is clean, that has a logical flow, that has...
has...
That
The hallmarks of standard, like, journeyman-style writing, at the very least, that's our job, right?
Like, to build those pipelines and to engineer them well enough to be able to accomplish those tasks.
In the face of all of the variants that LLMs offer, you know, they offer much power, but also much sloppiness.
And so our job is to marshal that sloppiness and leverage the power.

So, and the CMs or content managers, their job we view as, I would guess you'd refer to it, I'll use some editorial parlance because you've probably had enough experience to understand this, but they're more of a line editor, right?
They do take on some copy edit roles, but LLMs actually are pretty good about clean copy.
It'd be more like semantics or punctuation or, you know, maybe straying from a particular guideline that we gave it, things like that.
Then a managing editor would be like a true editor, right?
Exactly.
Executive editor or editor who would oversee those line editors.
And your job would be, of course, to then abstract that out one further.
Like, hey, where are you having problems as a CM?
You know, articulate to me what's going on.
Why are you not able to achieve the desired result?
Is it process?
Is it product?
Is it semantic?
Basically, like engineering the prompts or the context.
Like, let's talk it through.
let's figure out where the logical break is.
Now, we are attempting to, course, hire the CMs that are also good at those jobs.
So you're not alone out there, you know.
But that's the architecture of that delivery org.
Just so you get like a vibe of like, okay, what would I actually be doing, you know?
Yeah.
So in that context, I'd love to ask a couple of questions.
So I would love to talk or to hear from you about how you have like managed teams of writers, especially when it comes to deadlines, deliverables, feedback, you know.
Articulating feedback to them, because a good chunk of the role is articulation, right?
So just want to hear a little bit about that.
Sure.
So I've worked both within my own internal teams as having people, you know, reporting to me and being writers and editors for me.
And so the feedback loop is constant.
You know, we're on Slack with each other all the time.
So we're, you know, in constant communication internally.
Externally, with freelancers can be a little trickier.
Definitely, you're, you know, you're, you're not as aware of their, you know, their timeframes, their other constraints, whatever.
And so the communication pieces is ever more important in that conversation.
I've done a lot of it.
I, you know, in terms of managing deadlines and managing the feedback loop, I'm a stickler for, you know, a very organized person anyway.
But in, in work particularly, I'm a stickler for following.
I mean, I was, we.
We used Asana at TopTal, and that became my absolute preferred.
I mean, I just love Asana.
think it's wonderful.
I'm open to We're using a combination of linear and Airtable at the moment.
Yeah.
We may change.
I mean, we're a startup, so it will.
Right, exactly.
some point it'll change.
Yeah, I think Andy mentioned that to me, too, about Airtable being possibly not for the long term.
But anyway, I'm definitely a devotee of project management software.

But in terms of the conversation and the actual feedback and managing deadlines and freelancers, there have to be honest, from the get-go, conversations that start from the very beginning about expectations.
You certainly have to be somewhat flexible in the beginning, especially with new writers, making sure they understand the style, making sure they understand the expectations.
But really, I think it's...
It's communication.
I'm a big, like, talk to me.
Tell me what's going on.
Tell me what the blockers are.
How can I help remove the blockers to you getting this done?
Because a lot of times that's all you have to say.

mean, I had writers, you know, even internally, I had writers at TopTel, brilliant, you know, tech writers, brilliant editors could write about absolutely anything, but would get so, like, in their own heads that I wouldn't hear from them for days.

And I would say, like, wait a minute, you know, I know this is a tough topic, and I know you're doing all your research and interviewing people and digging up everything you can possibly find and make this, you know, happen.
But how can I clear the whatever is blocking you, whatever the cobwebs are, so you can get this done?
Because really, it needs to get done.

Like, you know, so it's a combination of, you know, strict taskmaster in terms of, like, letting, you know, if I've created a workflow in Asana, and I'm following the daily, it's daily check-ins, it's, you know, however, however often those
Check-ins need to happen.
I make sure they happen.
And honest communication about, you know, where is the blocker?
You know, just so you know, I am paying attention.
And that deadline is actually real.
And, you know, so of course, you, you know, sometimes you have to pad workflows to adapt to certain working styles.
But hopefully you don't allow too much padding because you, you do ultimately have to make a deadline.
And I'm, I've, my whole career, I've been very deadline driven and always made them with, issues.
So.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It makes sense.
I don't know if that answers your question.
No, no, it does.
That's, that's good.
I mean, the, the role definitely requires like a hip pocket understanding of like content quality, but of beyond that, it's more important.
And I think that, um, you're able to abstract that out into, you know, uh, helping other people understand.
And I think that's like the big thing that we're trying to hire for in this role is articulation.

Because the way that we build this product, and we are building this product, is that we have to understand where the process is broken, and where it is less than efficient, and articulate that clearly, and then that can be affected into a product improvement, right?
So it is a little bit of ad hoc product development, right?
Alongside, okay, and we also have to get client work out.
So that's, you know, that's the bit.
And I see that, that's what drew me to this, to be perfectly honest with you.
I, you know, I got a message from one of your recruiters, like, would you be interested in this role?

And normally, I don't pay a lot, like, you know, I get a lot of those, but I was really drawn to this because of that hybrid, because you're building something, and my question for you, I know we're not there yet, but I do have questions for you, which is- Yeah, yeah.
No, feel free.
Go ahead.
It's okay.
Yeah, I'm just really curious about the AI, you know, impact and how-
Obviously, you're building something.
Obviously, I know you've been at this for about a year and you're building something.
But just to finish the thought about what drew me to this, with my background in both really traditional media and digital media, and now I'm getting a flavor for AI.
I'm doing a little bit of freelance AI work.
I'm really curious to hear how you all, in the leadership of this company, where do you see the...
Where do you see what you're building going?
What is the opportunity and how has AI affected these clients that you're wooing and that you're working with?
What are they saying to you about using AI to create content for their marketing efforts?
What are they coming to you for?
Yeah.
Okay, so that's actually a lot of questions, which is fine.
Yeah, it's all good, because we definitely think about all of those and, you know, it's good that you're...
Thinking about them too.
Okay.
I'll start at the very highest level.
We are not a content company.
We are a workflows company, which means that we are building a system that can be adapted or useful for processes other than content generation.
So, and every, all of it will involve content at some point.
Words are the truck of knowledge, right?
And so you have to build that system first to understand, okay, how are we going to translate this into other worlds?
What other worlds would we translate it in?
I don't think it's too hard to see where the system we're building could be useful for sales.
It could be useful for HR.
could be useful for employee internal processes.
Basically the term that Marcel uses, which I think is, can be interpreted different ways, but I like still is we're organizing the messy middle.
And the way I think of it in terms of content, not to break it down.
Back to that, but that's just we're both content people.
It's like you mentioned the writers that you worked with that are super smart, and you know that they can turn out clean copy.
You give them an idea, they come back to you with really clean copy, maybe even some amazing logical flourishes.
You're like, I wouldn't even think of this angle.
This is great.
Really fantastic.
I think this is going to do well.
It hits the goals.
It also adds a bit of panache.
Like, wow, wonderful job.
Good job.
Hey, how did you do this?
I don't know, right?
Like their answer is like, I don't know, stuff went in, some things happened, and like this came out, right?
And so the people that are good at articulating that process are editors, right?
Like, that's why, you know, some writers can never be nor do they really want to be editors, which is fine.
But the people that are able to articulate the process of how they broke down the tasks, how they are.
Articulated the subtasks within those tasks, executed on them well, and then assembled them.
Those people are editors.
This is the people that can tell you, hey, you're missing a pin in this array here.
Otherwise, your argument is wobbly, right?
You need to set this up before you pay it off, whatever it is.
That articulation process in editorial is where we live a lot right now.
But we envision us being able to do that for many processes.
Things that are basically stuck in an employee's head when they exit the company for whatever reason.
Like, okay, cool, you have a really talented sales guy, and he's crushing it, securing deals, has really great customer relations techniques, all of that stuff.
That's wonderful, happy for that person, and happy that they will then be able to go on to another career or whatever.
But now...
Oh, like, okay, now we have to duplicate that process with someone else, right?

And so, like, articulating processes and being able to create workflows of those processes that couple it with human intervention and human interaction to add that extra bit of polish and keep building out the instruction set, like, that's the goal.

So the higher end goal here is we're a workflows company that will be able to articulate all of the various business processes that you do internally or wish you could do internally and productize them, give you a repeatable way to execute on them, and allow you to basically invest in really, like, high agency people who are good at articulation instead of just giving people, like, junior folks a playbook and expecting them to sort of, like, grind out the execution and then, you know, build those processes internally in their head, maybe over the...
The of a year or two years with your company and then leave, right?
Like that's the normal process.
So that's what we're trying to do at a very high level.
To bring it back down to earth, what we're doing right now is doing that same thing with content.

We are saying, hey, what is the process that content needs to go through between ideation or conceptualization and directionality, aka outlining or brief, then initial exploration, aka drafting, and then refinement, adapting to styling guidelines, fact checking, et cetera.
And then, I don't know, housekeeping, links and metadata and adherence to publishing SPACs and image generation and all of that stuff, right?
So like that process, taking that and breaking it down into its individual component parts.
Uh, finer and finer and finer detail until we finally get it right.
Right.
And that's basically the process we're doing now.
Got it.
So when you sign a client, you're working with them and you're, you are, you are creating the workflows that then ultimately they create the content.
You're not, you're not, you hand off, in other words.
No, no, no.
You are, we are, you are creating.
Well, we are full service.
Client comes with a problem, AKA, we would like more conversions.
We would like more traffic.
We would like this to happen.
Right.
And then we are like, we are in then in charge of coming up with a, and composing a strategic plan.
Great.
I understand this is your goals.
Here's our thoughts.
But according to what we know about this, we have a lot of experts.
We have a lot of other clients.
We kind of know that this is the right way to garner attention.
Of course, as you can imagine, a lot of these conversations are happening around geo at the moment.
Search engine optimization for LLMs.
And so we're like, hey, here's a strategy that we have tested and we have found some success with.
Now, this is still a dark art.
And most of our clients do understand that.
But they also don't really have the time or the money, frankly, to say, okay, I'm going to spin up a million and a half dollar a year content organization to experiment with geo, right?
And instead, we can say, no problem.
We'll charge you between $15,000 and $50,000 a month, whatever it is, to do all of that work for you.

The conceptualization, the exploration, the integration of our understanding and experimentation across 60 other clients, et cetera, to be able to help you to articulate or help you to execute on a plan that can at least get you somewhere.
And as we go, we will continue to monitor.
And all you have to do is touch base with us once every week to two weeks.
And you have garnered all the benefits of having anybody do this at the moment because nobody really knows how to do it, right?
And like that, that's kind of where we are at right now.
Basically, the integration or interaction with the client, as you can imagine, just roughly scales with how big the company is and how many staff, how many people they have on the staff.
So if they've got a big content org, all of a sudden you have six stakeholders in the Zoom call with you.
Okay, these things happen.

If it's a founder who's hired us directly, maybe they're on the call and they hand it off to their one marketing person and that's your point of contact and you just stay in touch and you do all that.

But the point of contact with the client can vary from, or the, not the point of contact, but the action the client takes can vary from reviewing drafts and giving feedback, which helps us to then improve the writing guidelines and in the instructions, we give the pipeline to like...
Hey, I think this is actually really good.
I don't really need to be involved much anymore.
Publish away.
And we just publish the articles, right?
So it can run the gamut, you know, obviously.
The more cooks you have in the kitchen, the more they have to justify their own jobs.
And so the more feedback you get, you know what I mean?
Same old story.
But yeah, that's the basic process.
So we generally would handle everything up to publishing.
Okay.
Okay.
So I'm just curious how you see this managing editor, I guess you call it technical managing editor.
I'm just curious how you see this role in the scheme of the company and what you're building.
Yeah, yeah.
So I think this dovetails with a question that I want to ask you before I answer that.
So the technical part of this, the technical managing editor, and the reason we added technical, because managing editor was the title of this role before we started our recent round of recruiting.
We added technical managing editor because we really explicitly want people who are...
Just really steeped in and up to their eyeballs in exploring AI tooling and workflows in their own work.

Even if it's not directly related to content, we just really want people who have a hunger for it because the process that we go through to build this is very much like, hey, we've got existing pipelines.
As an example, I'm currently shepherding three clients just because they're coming out of our sprint process.
And I want to see how they're doing and get a really ground level view of how the workflows are actually producing content for them.
Are they working well?
Right?
Like let's tune them like an engine so that we can then hand them off to a steady state team that can operate the machinery.
I'm able to produce with a VA and one CM about 30 pieces of content on Monday morning and be done for the week.
Because the pipelines are properly tuned.
And they have been massaged into getting the right results.
They have had great feedback.
Thankfully, these clients have been great about feedback, giving really detailed explanations of why they do or do not like a certain thing in an article, tonality.
We're working with a pre-launch women's supplement company at the moment, for example.
They make three supplement products, one for perimenopausal women, one for menopausal women, and one for postmenopausal women.
Articulating very clearly, like, the tone, the phrasing, the medical claims, all of that stuff, right?
That required some work.
Did not happen.
Like, our standard pipeline of just like, hey, take a topic and make a draft.
That was not great for this because it needed additional workflows to fact check against their databases of claims.
They had an ingredients list, for instance, for us, which was great because we had every...
The ingredient that's in their products, at what dosage, what the brand names of those ingredients are, etc.
And we had to build a workflow to check the article like, hey, you know, are the ingredients that are actually in this product being referenced here?
And are they correctly attributed to the symptoms they are addressing, whether it be inflammation or exhaustion or, you know, whatever they, why is it in the product?
And is it properly contextually integrated into the content?
And this is the work that a fact checker would do, right, or a copy editor would do, probably someone more senior than a copy editor, because it's a claims thing.
so therefore has legal implications and other things like that, right?
And that massaging work that we did with these clients then now enables us to execute on that, the content delivery in a very relaxed, calm manner.
Like we're able to get our deliverables out and then spend the rest of the week understanding the content staging, maybe doing a little strategizing, looking forward, polishing.
and the-yet came where code down In
Tweaking a little bit, getting it better.
And then we deliver usually once a week a batch of content to the client for review.
And so we're like four weeks ahead, which is, that's a good buffer.
People go on vacation.
They don't like some things.
got to retool them, you know, et cetera, right?
There are other pipelines in the company that are just getting their work done on time, right?

Like that's, it's very like variable at the moment, which is why the technical part of this exists, because we want our MEs to be really AI forward and to understand what it feels like to work with an LLM to either in the Socratic method or with a, with a workflow tool, like, like a make.com or a Zapier or an N8N or something like that, or on a very ground level way, you know, chatting back and forth with any LLM, whether it be, you know, Gemini or, or perplexity or ChatTBT or, or Claude, um, we use Claude and open.
OpenAI backends for a lot of our processes.
We also use other researchers similar to Perplexity.
We actually found Perplexity to not be quite good enough for what we do.
It's good.
It's very good.
But there are others that are better for our specific use case, other researchers.
But it's not required, of course, that we're not hiring for AI engineers for this role.
But all of our processes involve this interaction.
And therefore, the people that are going to be helping CMs articulate what is wrong, what they need improvements on, they just need to have an understanding of inputs and outputs.
What do you give an LLM?
What are the right semantic choices to make?
How do you refine those semantics, aka prompt engineering, right?
And then also, what context are you giving the LLM to work with?
That is context engineering.
Okay, we've got a context artifact that's called the company context, right?
And that being...
It is essentially an executive dossier about the company.
What does it do?
What are all of its products?
Who are its competitors?
How has it positioned itself in the market?
All of this.
So that when we do write about the company, aka send the pipeline off to go generate an article, if we are doing, for instance, a competitor comparison article, it knows how to position that company.
It knows the way the company positions itself.
But that document did not just coalesce out of like one fired prompt of perplexity to do research on this company.
We can even have conversations with the company, with its stakeholders.
Sometimes we'll do like SME interviews.
We'll do customer interviews.
We do obviously Fathom calls with the client every week.
We can integrate feedback.
We will chop it up with them.
They'll give us information about the way that they're about to position their product soon.
They're like, hey, we're deprecating this and we're going after this.
We're pivoting the company this way.
Great.
That needs to be engineered, aka rewritten into the company context so that we continue to understand it.
Generally speaking, we do that work with LLMs as well, right?
We'll take the body of new information we have and have an LLM build a framework for integrating that into the new context, which I'm not trying to make it sound more complex than it is.
It's just having a conversation, but at the same time, you need to know how to have that conversation.
And having that conversation with humans is wonderful.
It's a wonderful background and skill set, which I'm sure you have had many, many times, thousands of times, which is great.
But then being able to translate that into how LLMs expect it to be heard is super important for us.
So that's like a long way of me asking you, can you talk a little bit about how you work with LLMs and other AI tooling and your personal work or work work?
Yes.
In work work, it was just starting when I was at TopTal.
So we really weren't, we were skeptical of it.
We were told at first not ever to use it because then we were told use it, you know.
So, so we got a lot of mixed messages, but once.
I'm insatiably curious and fascinated by what I've seen that it can do and the potential for teaching it to think better and for teaching it to think like a writer.
You know, again, I say that, you know, it's really just a caveat.
For the most part, I've never been intimidated by any technical advance.
So I've always dove head first in whatever capacity, both, you know, in my personal, you know, freelance work and my professional work.
So it doesn't intimidate me.

## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager screen for Technical Managing Editor role at growthx.ai](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=6.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role focuses on articulating/improving AI-driven content workflows (60%) and overseeing client deliverables (40%)](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
  - [Company building scalable AI content generation system, aiming to expand into other business processes](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1327.0)
  - [Seeking candidates with strong editorial background and enthusiasm for AI/LLM experimentation](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1597.0)
  - [Position requires balancing client needs, AI capabilities, and quality content production at scale](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=2340.0)

## Topics

### Company Overview and Role Context

  - [growthx.ai is building AI-driven workflow systems, starting with content generation](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1327.0)
  - [Aim to expand into other business processes (sales, HR, internal processes)](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1334.0)
  - [Current focus: refining content pipelines from ideation to publication](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1527.0)
  - [Technical Managing Editor role involves:](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
      - [Articulating/improving AI workflows (60%)](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1224.0)
      - [Overseeing client deliverables (40%)](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
      - [Bridging gap between AI capabilities and client needs](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=701.0)

### Client Service Model

  - [Full-service approach: strategy development, content creation, and delivery](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
  - [Clients range from founders to large organizations with multiple stakeholders](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1522.0)
  - [Pricing: $15,000-$50,000/month for comprehensive content services](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1449.0)
  - [Leveraging AI to produce high volume (e.g., 30 pieces/week) with minimal human oversight](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1667.0)
  - [Focus on establishing "beachhead" of quality content for clients to build upon](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1425.0)

### AI and LLM Integration

  - [Using Claude and OpenAI backends for core processes](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1860.0)
  - [Emphasis on prompt engineering and context engineering](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1901.0)
  - [Building company-specific context documents for accurate content generation](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1912.0)
  - [Integrating client feedback and new information into AI systems](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1949.0)

### Candidate Background and Skills

  - [Strong editorial background crucial (e.g., managing writers, meeting deadlines)](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=409.0)
  - [Enthusiasm for AI/LLM experimentation highly valued](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1597.0)
  - [Ability to articulate complex processes and improve workflows](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=960.0)
  - [Experience with project management tools (e.g., Asana) beneficial](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=786.0)
  - [Teaching background seen as advantageous for "training" AI systems](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=2220.0)

### Content Quality and Scale

  - [AI-generated content aimed at being factually accurate and reliably written](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=587.0)
  - [Focus on high volume and establishing internet authority for clients](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1439.0)
  - [Balancing scale with quality to meet varying client needs](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=2400.0)
  - [Allowing clients to focus on high-value content while AI handles bulk production](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=2488.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Hiring team to review conversation and make decision on next steps](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1597.0)
  - [Candidate to await feedback on potential advancement in hiring process](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=1597.0)
  - [Both parties to consider fit based on role requirements and candidate's background](https://fathom.video/share/229iiBSz8MxAGbcEFmw--d9-j23H67En?tab=summary&timestamp=2569.0)
